The foruser value determines which user sees the session in their gallery/session list and has owner-level permissions. It must correspond to a valid user account on the inSign instance. If not specified, the session is created under the authenticated API user. This is essential for multi-user environments where sessions must be assigned to specific consultants.

Client-side redirect URL that the user's browser navigates to when the signing session is closed or completed. This root-level session property enables seamless integration with the calling application by returning the user to a specific page after signing. The session ID is typically appended as a query parameter. Used in conjunction with exit.available and extern.exit.enabled to provide navigation back to the integrating application. If not set, the user remains on the inSign page after session completion.

The email address of the session owner (consultant/advisor). This root-level session property is used for email communications related to the session, such as notifications about external signer actions (signing completed, rejected, delegated). The email is also used as the default sender address if senderEmail is not explicitly configured. May be displayed in the session UI and included in email templates sent to external signers.

Server-side webhook URL that receives automated notifications about session status changes. Unlike callbackURL (which is a browser redirect), this URL is called server-to-server by the inSign backend when events occur such as session completion, external signer actions, or document finalization. The request includes the session ID and status information. This is the primary mechanism for backend integration, allowing the calling system to react to signing events programmatically without polling for status.

The HTTP method used when calling the serverSidecallbackURL webhook. Supported values: 'GET' or 'POST'. Default: 'GET'. When set to 'POST', the session status data is sent in the request body rather than as URL query parameters. POST is recommended for security (parameters not visible in server logs) and for transmitting larger payloads. The content type of POST requests is controlled by serversideCallbackContenttype.

The Content-Type header used for POST requests to the serverSidecallbackURL webhook. Common values: 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' (default) or 'application/json'. Only relevant when serversideCallbackMethod is set to 'POST'. This determines how the callback payload is encoded in the request body. Use 'application/json' if your webhook endpoint expects JSON-formatted data.

Controls whether biometric signature data (pen pressure, stroke velocity, timing) is embedded into the signed PDF document. Default: false. When enabled, handwritten signature biometric data is stored within the PDF for forensic analysis purposes. This data can be used by handwriting experts to verify the authenticity of a signature. The biometric data is encrypted within the PDF and requires specialized tools to extract. Enabling this strengthens the evidentiary value of AES signatures but increases the PDF file size.

When enabled, all form fields (text inputs, checkboxes) in the document become mandatory and must be filled in before the signing process can be completed. Default: false. This is a root-level session property that enforces complete form filling across all documents in the session. Unlike allSignaturesRequired which targets signature fields, this property targets non-signature form fields. Useful for contracts and applications where every field must be completed for legal or compliance reasons.

Controls whether an audit report (protocol/log) is generated for the signing session. Default: false, Recommended: true. When enabled, the system creates a detailed audit trail document recording all actions taken during the signing session, including timestamps, IP addresses, signature placements, and user interactions. The audit report is added as an additional document to the session and can be downloaded alongside the signed documents. This provides legal evidence of the signing process and is recommended for compliance-sensitive workflows.

Pre-enables external signing mode for this session at creation time. Default: false. When set to true, the session is immediately configured for external signing without the consultant needing to manually activate it in the UI. This is a root-level session property distinct from the guiProperties extern.available (which controls UI visibility of the external action). Use this when the session is intended exclusively for external signers and should skip the consultant interaction step entirely.

Controls whether the signing process is automatically completed when the external signer finishes signing all required fields. Default: false. When enabled, the session transitions to a completed state immediately after the external signer places their last required signature, without requiring the consultant to manually finalize the process. This enables fully automated external signing workflows where no consultant intervention is needed after the initial session setup.

Controls whether signed documents are automatically sent to the external signer via email upon session completion. Default: false. When enabled, the system automatically emails the finalized signed documents to the external signer after the process is completed. This ensures the external signer receives a copy of the signed documents without requiring manual intervention. Works in conjunction with externCompleteOnFinish for fully automated end-to-end external signing workflows.

Unique identifier for a document within the signing session. This document-level property is set when adding a document to the session configuration. The ID is used to reference this specific document in API calls for status checks, downloads, and configuration. It must be unique within the session. Common conventions include sequential IDs (doc1, doc2) or descriptive identifiers matching the document purpose.

The URL from which the inSign server downloads the PDF document to include in the signing session. This document-level property specifies a publicly accessible or server-reachable URL pointing to the PDF file. The server fetches the document at session creation time. Alternative: use the 'file' property to provide the document content directly as a base64-encoded string or input stream. The URL must be accessible from the inSign server (not the client browser).

The document content provided directly as base64-encoded data or an input stream, instead of fetching from a URL. This document-level property is an alternative to fileURL for cases where the document is not available via a public URL. When using the REST API, provide the PDF content as a base64-encoded string. When using the Java API, an InputStream can be passed directly. Use this when documents are stored in a private system, generated dynamically, or when URL-based access is not feasible.

Controls whether the system scans the PDF document for signature tag placeholders and automatically creates signature fields at their positions. Default: false. When enabled, the server parses the PDF content looking for predefined text markers (signature tags) and replaces them with interactive signature fields. This allows document templates to define signature positions using simple text placeholders rather than requiring programmatic field placement via the API. Commonly used with PDF templates generated from word processors or document management systems.

Controls whether this document must be signed before the session can be completed. Default: false. This document-level property makes signing mandatory for the specific document. If any document with mustbesigned=true has unsigned required signature fields, the finish/complete action is blocked. Use this to enforce signing on critical documents (contracts, agreements) while allowing other documents in the session (information sheets, terms) to remain unsigned.

Controls whether this document must be read and acknowledged before the session can proceed. Default: false. When enabled, the user must scroll through or view the entire document and confirm reading via a checkbox or acknowledgment button. The confirmation text is specified via mustbereadText. This is used for mandatory-read documents like terms and conditions, privacy policies, or risk disclosures where legal compliance requires proof of document review before signing.

The confirmation text displayed to the user when mustberead is enabled on a document. This text appears as the label of the checkbox or acknowledgment button that the user must click to confirm they have read the document. Example: 'I have read and accept the terms and conditions'. The text should clearly state what the user is acknowledging. Supports localization - provide text in the appropriate language matching the session locale.
